description: A working log for research taste: each row starts with a problem, names the prior art, makes a forecast, defines what would falsify it, and points to the cheapest next test.

items.problem: AI infra maps often jump from GPUs to model serving, skipping the cluster substrate: fabrics, collectives, schedulers, and distributed runtimes.
items.why_now: Bigger training and inference clusters make networking, scheduling, and runtime behavior first-order constraints, not implementation details.
prior_art: Ray and Anyscale expose distributed Python and AI workloads.
NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, Slurm, Run:ai, KubeRay, SkyPilot, Broadcom, and Arista define the practical cluster surface.
items.forecast: The next useful AI infrastructure map needs explicit cluster substrate entries, but not a new taxonomy axis yet.
items.falsifier: Users still cannot place concrete bottlenecks like all-reduce, GPU allocation, or east-west fabric after the additions.
items.cheap_test: Ask three infra people to classify a failed training run by bottleneck: model, data, scheduler, collective, or fabric.
items.belief_update: Better coverage; next gap is workload economics by bottleneck.

items.problem: Public miners advertise gross MW, but AI buyers and credit funds need critical-IT MW, retrofit cost, delivery risk, and financeability.
items.why_now: AI capacity demand is colliding with bitcoin-mining power campuses, while public filings reveal enough fragments to build named-site derates.
prior_art: Independent Engineer reports and rating-agency diligence exist, but arrive late and are not public.
SemiAnalysis-style public teardowns show that technical infrastructure research can become a distribution wedge.
items.forecast: A named public derate, not a generic landing page, is the credibility engine for a paid research wedge.
items.falsifier: Credit funds and capacity buyers say existing IE, broker, and internal engineering workflows already answer the question cheaply.
items.cheap_test: Publish one named teardown and send it to 30 funds, analysts, operators, and technical advisors.
items.belief_update: Still attractive, but only if distribution starts before more polishing.

items.problem: Most eval products sell dashboards, but the research skill is reading failures, sorting them into piles, and updating the benchmark.
items.why_now: Agent products are leaving demo land, and teams need eval loops that catch regressions, tool misuse, prompt drift, and unsafe actions.
prior_art: LangSmith, Patronus AI, OpenAI tracing, Arize/Phoenix, and Braintrust cover parts of the loop.
Andrew Ng and Karpathy both emphasize reading raw failures before optimizing metrics.
items.forecast: The durable wedge is workflow around failure taxonomies and review queues, not another aggregate score.
items.falsifier: Teams will not pay for explicit failure review workflows because they prefer existing observability dashboards and custom spreadsheets.
items.cheap_test: Take 100 failures from one agent workflow, classify them manually, then see whether the top two piles suggest product changes.
items.belief_update: Good fit for public writing and small tooling, not yet a standalone startup thesis.

items.problem: On-device ML is split across runtimes, chips, operating systems, model formats, app constraints, and distribution channels.
items.why_now: Apple, Google, Qualcomm, PyTorch ExecuTorch, LiteRT, Core ML, MLX, and local LLM tooling are making edge inference a real deployment path.
prior_art: Core ML, MLX, LiteRT, ONNX Runtime Mobile, ExecuTorch, llama.cpp, MLC LLM, and Qualcomm AI Hub.
Mobile and embedded developers care about latency, battery, privacy, package size, and hardware-specific acceleration.
items.forecast: The best opportunity is a compatibility/evaluation layer for edge deployment, not another runtime.
items.falsifier: Platform-native tooling solves enough of the workflow that cross-platform edge AI tooling is only useful to consultants.
items.cheap_test: Deploy the same small model across Core ML, LiteRT, ONNX Runtime Mobile, and ExecuTorch; compare latency, size, accuracy drift, and integration pain.
items.belief_update: Needs a benchmark artifact before judging opportunity quality.
